runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.
citations: Chirinko (2008), 'σ: The Long and Short of It,' Journal of Macroeconomics — surveys a wide range of estimates and concludes the elasticity is likely in the 0.4–0.6 range.
Antràs (2004), 'Is the U.S. Aggregate Production Function Cobb-Douglas?' — finds evidence for σ < 1, around 0.6–0.8.
Karabarbounis & Neiman (2014), 'The Global Decline of the Labor Share' — argue σ > 1 to explain labor share trends, around 1.25, representing the higher-end view.
runs.reasoningSummary: The empirical literature on the capital-labor substitution elasticity is contested. A major survey by Chirinko (2008) concludes estimates cluster in the 0.4–0.6 range for the U.S. aggregate, while time-series and cross-country studies (e.g., Antràs 2004) typically find 0.5–0.8. Some more recent work (Karabarbounis & Neiman 2014) argues σ > 1 is needed to explain falling labor shares, but this is disputed. The Cobb-Douglas benchmark of σ = 1 is a common baseline. Balancing these, my median belief is ~0.7, with genuine uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.2 at the 5th–95th percentile range, reflecting both the Chirinko-style low estimates and the possibility that σ is near or slightly above unity.

A major survey by Chirinko (2008) concludes estimates cluster in the 0.4\u20130.6 range for the U.S. aggregate, while time-series and cross-country studies (e.g., Antr\u00e0s 2004) typically find 0.5\u20130.8. Some more recent work (Karabarbounis & Neiman 2014) argues \u03c3 > 1 is needed to explain falling labor shares, but this is disputed. The Cobb-Douglas benchmark of \u03c3 = 1 is a common baseline. Balancing these, my median belief is ~0.7, with genuine uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.2 at the 5th\u201395th percentile range, reflecting both the Chirinko-style low estimates and the possibility that \u03c3 is near or slightly above unity."}

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.
citations: Antràs (2004) – 'Is the U.S. Aggregate Production Function Cobb-Douglas?' finds σ ≈ 0.6–0.8 using postwar U.S. data.
Chirinko (2008) – survey in Journal of Macroeconomics concludes σ is in the range 0.4–0.6 for most empirical studies.
Karabarbounis & Neiman (2014) – estimate σ slightly above 1 to explain the falling labor share, but their methodology is contested.
runs.reasoningSummary: The empirical literature on the aggregate capital-labor elasticity of substitution for the U.S. is substantial but somewhat dispersed. The classic Cobb-Douglas assumption imposes σ = 1, but most modern empirical estimates using time-series or cross-country variation tend to find σ below 1, with many studies clustering around 0.5–0.8. Chirinko's 2008 survey is influential in anchoring beliefs around 0.4–0.6, while Antràs finds values closer to 0.6–0.8 for the U.S. Some studies (e.g., Karabarbounis & Neiman) argue for σ > 1 to explain the declining labor share, but this is a minority view in the aggregate production-function literature. The long-run elasticity is generally thought to be higher than short-run estimates. My median is 0.7 with meaningful uncertainty on both sides, and the 95th percentile extends to 1.2 to allow for the Cobb-Douglas benchmark and above.

The classic Cobb-Douglas assumption imposes \u03c3 = 1, but most modern empirical estimates using time-series or cross-country variation tend to find \u03c3 below 1, with many studies clustering around 0.5\u20130.8. Chirinko's 2008 survey is influential in anchoring beliefs around 0.4\u20130.6, while Antr\u00e0s finds values closer to 0.6\u20130.8 for the U.S. Some studies (e.g., Karabarbounis & Neiman) argue for \u03c3 > 1 to explain the declining labor share, but this is a minority view in the aggregate production-function literature. The long-run elasticity is generally thought to be higher than short-run estimates. My median is 0.7 with meaningful uncertainty on both sides, and the 95th percentile extends to 1.2 to allow for the Cobb-Douglas benchmark and above."}

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.
citations: Antràs (2004) 'Is the U.S. Aggregate Production Function Cobb-Douglas? New Estimates of the Elasticity of Substitution,' Contributions to Macroeconomics — estimates σ around 0.6–0.7 using postwar U.S. data.
Chirinko (2008) 'σ: The Long and Short of It,' Journal of Macroeconomics — surveys the literature and concludes the weight of evidence places σ in the range 0.4–0.6.
Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) 'The Global Decline of the Labor Share,' QJE — use σ > 1 as a key assumption but acknowledge the empirical debate around values between 0.5 and 1.
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on the aggregate capital-labor elasticity of substitution for the U.S. is large and contested. Early estimates assumed Cobb-Douglas (σ = 1). More careful time-series and cross-sectional work (Antràs 2004, Chirinko 2008) tends to find σ below 1, often in the 0.4–0.8 range, with a central tendency around 0.6–0.7. Some macro models calibrate σ slightly above 1 to match the declining labor share (Karabarbounis-Neiman). Given the preponderance of sub-unitary estimates from careful empirical work and the reasonable possibility of values close to or modestly above 1, I center my belief at 0.7 with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.2 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

Some macro models calibrate \u03c3 slightly above 1 to match the declining labor share (Karabarbounis-Neiman). Given the preponderance of sub-unitary estimates from careful empirical work and the reasonable possibility of values close to or modestly above 1, I center my belief at 0.7 with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.2 at the 5th\u201395th percentile range."}

citations: Antràs (2004, QJE) – estimates σ ≈ 0.6–0.7 using U.S. aggregate data with capital-augmenting technical change

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on aggregate U.S. capital-labor substitution is split. I center my median at 0.7, reflecting the weight of direct production-function estimation, with a right-skewed distribution allowing for values approaching or exceeding 1.

citations: Antràs (2004, QJE) – estimates sigma around 0.6–0.7 using U.S. aggregate data, finding it below unity.

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature is divided but the center of gravity for aggregate U.S. estimates sits below 1. Some cross-country and industry studies (e.g., Karabarbounis & Neiman) imply sigma > 1 to fit trends in capital's share, but these are contested. Cobb-Douglas (sigma = 1) is a common benchmark but not strongly supported by direct estimation. I center my belief at 0.7 with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3–1.2 at the 90% interval, reflecting genuine empirical disagreement about identification and time horizon.

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on the aggregate capital-labor elasticity of substitution for the U.S. is large but reasonably convergent. Chirinko's survey finds a central tendency around 0.4–0.6; other studies using factor shares and investment data place it closer to 0.7–0.8. Some recent work tied to the falling labor share implies σ > 1, but that remains contested. I place my median at 0.7, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.1 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.
citations: Chirinko (2008, Journal of Economic Literature) — surveys literature and argues central tendency is around 0.4–0.6, well below 1.
Antràs (2004, QJE) — estimates σ ≈ 0.6–0.8 using cross-country data distinguishing short- and long-run.
Karabarbounis & Neiman (2014, QJE) — use declining labor share to back out σ > 1, though contested.
runs.reasoningSummary: The empirical literature on the aggregate capital-labor elasticity of substitution is wide-ranging. Early studies (Cobb-Douglas assumption) set σ = 1, but post-1990 evidence generally finds σ < 1. Chirinko's influential 2008 survey concludes a central tendency of roughly 0.4–0.6. Time-series and cross-sectional studies (Antràs 2004, Oberfield & Raval 2021) cluster in the 0.5–0.8 range for long-run estimates. A minority strand (Karabarbounis & Neiman 2014) argues σ > 1 to explain the declining labor share, but this is contested. My median is 0.7, reflecting a slight upward tilt from the Chirinko consensus toward longer-run estimates, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.1 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

Early studies (Cobb-Douglas assumption) set \u03c3 = 1, but post-1990 evidence generally finds \u03c3 < 1. Chirinko's influential 2008 survey concludes a central tendency of roughly 0.4\u20130.6. Balancing these, my median is 0.7 with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.1 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

Time-series and cross-sectional studies (Antr\u00e0s 2004, Oberfield & Raval 2021) cluster around 0.5\u20130.8. A minority of studies using factor-share trends (Karabarbounis & Neiman 2014) infer values above 1. Cobb-Douglas (\u03c3 = 1) remains a common benchmark. Balancing these, my median is 0.7 with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.1 at the 5th\u201395th percentile range."}

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Elasticity of substitution between capital and labor
- Definition: Percent change in the capital-labor ratio from a 1 percent change in the marginal rate of technical substitution between capital and labor in a CES production function.
- Target interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate private-sector production
- Population/context: U.S. aggregate private-sector production
- Units: elasticity

Task:
1. Use exactly the target interpretation above. In `interpretation`, restate it briefly.
2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.
citations: Chirinko (2008, Journal of Economic Literature) — surveys the literature and argues estimates cluster below 1, around 0.4–0.6
Antràs (2004, QJE) — estimates σ ≈ 0.6 for U.S. aggregate using capital-share variation
Karabarbounis & Neiman (2014, QJE) — assume/estimate elasticity somewhat above 1 to explain declining labor share
runs.reasoningSummary: The empirical literature on aggregate capital-labor substitution is wide and contested. Chirinko's 2008 survey concludes most credible estimates lie below 1, with a central tendency around 0.4–0.7. Antràs (2004) and similar time-series studies yield estimates near 0.5–0.7. However, some macro studies (e.g., Karabarbounis & Neiman) require σ > 1 to explain the secular decline in the labor share, pulling the upper tail higher. Cobb-Douglas (σ = 1) remains a common calibration benchmark. Balancing these, my median belief is around 0.8, reflecting a distribution centered below 1 but with meaningful probability mass at and above 1. The p05 of 0.4 captures the lower-bound estimates from careful time-series identification, and the p95 of 1.3 captures macro studies that need σ > 1.

Chirinko's 2008 survey concludes most credible estimates lie below 1, with a central tendency around 0.4\u20130.7. Antr\u00e0s (2004) and similar time-series studies yield estimates near 0.5\u20130.7. However, some macro studies (e.g., Karabarbounis & Neiman) require \u03c3 > 1 to explain the secular decline in the labor share, pulling the upper tail higher. Cobb-Douglas (\u03c3 = 1) remains a common calibration benchmark. Balancing these, my median belief is around 0.8, reflecting a distribution centered below 1 but with meaningful probability mass at and above 1. The p05 of 0.4 captures the lower-bound estimates from careful time-series identification, and the p95 of 1.3 captures macro studies that need \u03c3 > 1."}
